speaker: interviewer
text: Karliner, K-A-R-L-I-N-E-R. This is roll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were cruising off of Miami Beach at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the captain came close to Miami Beach. I remember it very well-- Miami Beach-- because I was so impressed to see the coconuts, trees, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You could actually see the-- you were that close?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And I found out later on that the captain wanted to-- that there was boats down at night. Whoever wants to come ashore, he would have done it. Well, just at the time, we-- all of a sudden, we saw some Coast Guard cutter came in there. And with their loudspeaker, they said, you are much too close to shore. You better get out to the international zone, whatever it is. So the captain had to leave. Well, I tell you. Believe me when I tell you that. I was about 11 and a half, 12 years old, on that ship. And I was so impressed with Miami Beach. I said, someday I have to come back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it took a little time. Well, the-- we couldn't land in any countries. Nobody would let us in. And naturally, I found out afterward Goebbels, the propaganda minister from Germany, made a big affair. It shows you that nobody wants the Jews in the world. In meantime, we-- the food got short. Water got short. And we had to-- the ship had to go back to Germany. I don't have to tell you the atmosphere on the ship at that time. Everybody was a worry. And rumor got around that they might do something. Some people might do something--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ship. I was too sh-- too small at the time to find out. But I hear afterwards that some people wanted to put a fire on the ship or something like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The captain was worried, too. And he putting out the guard, the sailors. Instead a single sailor for checking-- I mean patrolling-- he put two or three guys out on patrol.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was generally the attitude among the a-- like your parents? Were they just helpless or angry or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely helpless.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely-- From everybody. We didn't know what to do. And you're on a ship. What can we do?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- we're hoping that we get some more help from the outside. And we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting messages from the outside then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. We got telegrams. I used to love to go in the telephone-- the telegraph room and listen to them and the rumors and everything else. But nothing happens. Also, I want to come back to the captain. The captain was very, very understanding. We, as children, we used to run around the places. We used to go to the-- I call it, the, the the captain's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Crews' quarters you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crews' quarters and the, the-- all the equipment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bridge-- and played around. And he was always very friendly to us. There's some peoples went to him and talked to him. I heard afterward. And he was always very, very sympathetic to us, to the people. He went back slowly. And hopefully, there's somebody else might do something. And about two days before we came to Germany, we got a telegram that four countries would let us in. We'd be divided. There was Holland, Belgium, France, and England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So naturally, all of a sudden, we had hope again. We start to dance. We had some movies. And everybody was happy that we don't go back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were divided. I think each, each country took in about 220, 250 people. We didn't have much choice where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how the division was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did your parents have a choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not that they had a choice. I know only that we-- our-- we came to France [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were taken into France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some other friends went to England. My uncle went to Belgium. But in last moment, he were allowed to change it to come with us to France. We were-- another German ship came next to the St. Louis in Antwerp. The people to Belgium and Holland were disembarked in Antwerp, Belgium. And the people going to France and England were put on another sh-- German ship. Next, day we arrived in [? Bouillon ?], France. We disembarked. And there was some Jewish organization waiting for us. And the French, too, they didn't know what to do with us. They didn't want to make a big fuss. And so they put five, six family in each small villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they spread you around the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely-- and there was a Jewish organization for children. France was a [? floater ?] country. Refugees came from there from Austria, Poland, and so on. And there was an organization which was formed in the early 1900s in Russia, Jewish family. And when the revolution came, they left there and went to Poland, and then from Poland to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH SPEECH]-- that means [FRENCH SPEECH]. And then they went to France. My parents felt that they cannot handle four children. And they decided to put us in the children home, my brother, myself, and my little sister. My other sister was around 16, 17 years old. Felt they will take her with them. But we went to a children home outside Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been very difficult for your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. But for us, it was-- I enjoyed it tremendously. It was something completely new. My parents went to a small village outside [INAUDIBLE]-- that was in southwestern France-- with a few other Jewish family from the trip. They got a little apartment. First, they stayed in a hotel. And then they got a little apartment. And I think the Jewish organization gave them some money to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was the Joint, from the Joint at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was somewhere in June, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did-- could you communicate with your parents from the children's home to where they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by writing-- we write-- wrote each other all the time. I was about that time-- oh, you know? OK. September 1st, 1939, the war broke out with Poland. [? Still, ?] [? it wasn't ?] a lot of things, but in September 3rd, 1939, which was my birthday, the Second World War started with France and England. I remember very well. And it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember happening to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, I happened to always run around with the German when they had maneuvers in the fall by us. And I know the German mentality and the discipline and the, the tanks and everything else. When I came to France and I saw the French army, they still had weapons from the First World War. And I was very down, what I saw. Well, I was right. About a month or two later, Paris was bombarded. And the organization, the-- from the [FRENCH SPEECH], they decided to send the children home, change them to Southern France. They bought a small chateau, a castle, which were not prepared for us whatsoever. But they send us down to the that place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the?

speaker: interviewer
text: The little town you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And the chateau, chateau, Chateau [INAUDIBLE] was there. We got there. And it was cold. It was sometimes in November. We had children from three years old to about 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you? Do you-- how many children were you [? talking? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: In this, in this chateau, this castle, we were about 90 children, all ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brother and sister, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we had to-- we, since we were the oldest, we had to start to work, clean up the place there, and so on. Some of us, some of the kids went afterward to school. It was about three kilometers to the village. So we had to walk down early in the morning and at night, come back there. There was a lot up, up the hill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't speak no French whatsoever, you know? We had to learn French. And they felt that, for the older like us, the 13 year old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me. I have to come back on something. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, was my 13 birthday. I want to have my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris. We were about 15 boys had bar mitzvah in one shot. There was also a rel-- religious home for the children. And they had also some people there. And the mayor of that village on the out-- outskirts from Paris, was [INAUDIBLE]. And she was Jewish, the mayor of that city. And she came also to the synagogue. We didn't had much to say. We just had our [INAUDIBLE] on the Torah. And I remember she gave us each one a present. We got the [INAUDIBLE], a [INAUDIBLE], and a hammer or a saw, because there was a-- we had to work in the, in the children home. My parents even couldn't come to the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- your parents did-- were not-- they couldn't get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't get there. And I still remember the bar mitzvah my brother had in my hometown, which was elaborate. All the relatives came and so on. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But now you were in the South of France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I was in the South of France. And they decided that all, all the people like us, 13-year-old, should go to work. Most of the people in the village were drafted in the army. And there were a lot of the work opening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody in the bakery there needed a helper. And they sent me down to work in the bakery. I remember the first time I went to work, I-- they gave me a piece of paper. And they tell me what it means-- water, flour, yeast, and so on. That's the way I started to-- That's how you learned to speak French and to be a baker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, speak the French and be a baker. Most the time, I had to cut wood because it was heating with woods and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the French welcoming? I mean, did you feel like you all were welcome there? Were they kind to you as children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice. I wouldn't say they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they were at war, too, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But the-- you know, for example, when the war broke out, the French interned my father and my uncle because they were German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had to work in the salt mine in France. My uncle was sent to Southern France. And they put him in a camp to be a soldier or something like that. But since he was still a German, they were not sure of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed with my sister in the village there. Anyway, my father got very sick. He got, got kidney problem in that salt mine. And they sent him back home afterward.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle stayed in the camp there, which I'm going to come back afterward. In the meantime, I was in the village. And my brother worked as a carpenter. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your little sister was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was in a upstairs. She went to school. One day, maybe a year after-- 1940, 1940-- My parents came to visit us. And she saw the condition of the children home, which was not elaborate or something like this. They took my little sister with them back. And my brother and myself, we stayed. Well, I don't have to tell you in the, in the late 40's, the German invaded Holland, Belgium, and France. And again, we were occupied by the Germans. My parents were in the occupied zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of France-- we were in a non-occupied zone. At the beginning, it didn't make much difference. I mean, [? anyway, ?] there was not too much problem there. Because the French, most of time, took over those things. My boss's son where I worked was taken prisoner and was in Germany in the prison camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. He was a Frenchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a Frenchman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, I worked with his father-- very, very nice person. And his daughter-in-law runs the store. And she had a little girl who was two years old, and myself. Well, at the beginning, like I said, I walked down at 3 o'clock in the morning to go to work and came back at 5 o'clock in the evening. Finally, they gave me a bicycle. So it was easier. And then after a year or so, they let me stay up in the attic. I stayed up in the attic. I was working there for about a year and a half. And one-- after a year, my boss said, I going to give you a one-week vacation. And I wanted to see my parents. We were not allowed to send any letters from the occupied zone [INAUDIBLE], only postcards so everybody can read them. So, I wrote to my parents, Uncle Herbie wants to see and Aunt Elsa, which was my other sister, to make them understand that I want to come to visit them. I waited, waited for an answer-- never got an answer. You know, when you're 14, 15 years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, to heck with them, I'm going-- no paper, just a map. And I took the train to a certain way. And then I walked to cross the border. I crossed the border at night from the occupied zone to the other side. And I walked about 30 kilometers on a Sunday. And I arrived to the village. I-- there was no name or numbers on the street there, you know, in the village. But I know more or less where the, the street number was-- the street was there, but not the numbers. So I didn't know where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that Sunday, I passed by, walked by. And there was a curve there. And my mother worked a little bit for somebody, a haberdasher for some pants-- she made some pants-- also a Jewish, Polish-Jewish guy. And she was on the window. And she saw me passing by. And she screamed. She said, that's Herbie. My father jumped. He said, oh you're crazy. He's there, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I passed by. And then she looked out the window. But I went over here from the curve. In the meantime, I find out, I asked somebody where-- and they told me. And I came back. So naturally, we are so happy. [? Instead ?] to stay a week, I stayed two weeks. In that two weeks, twice, we found out that some German soldiers came to the village. And we run outside, run in a field. Because we are afraid something might happen to us. But it was nothing happened. And they called us back. They had very good friends-- my parents had very good French people, friends. And I decided to go back--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the south.

speaker: interviewee
text: To work, to, to the kin-- children home, to work. But you know how Yiddish mommy mama are. They-- I told her that I slept in the woods coming there. And she said, don't sleep in the wood. She gave me some extra money. She said, after you cross the border, make sure in the first village you go, go to a-- take a room in a hotel. So like a good Jewish boy, I listened to my mother. [LAUGHS] I [? went ?] in the next village. And I took a room. And the, the, proprietor asked me if I had some paper. I said, no. So she gave me the room. 3 o'clock in the morning, the French gendarme came and put me in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you didn't have papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No papers-- and they asked me where I came from and what I doing here. Naturally, I didn't want to say where I came from or who or other things. I just gave him the address of the children home. And they got in touch with the children home. They said, yes, that's me, and blah, blah, blah. So next day, they put me on the bus and sent me home.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did send you home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent-- they sent me home. Except that a few weeks later, I was called to the court. And I had to pay a fine because I was not allowed to travel without any papers. If you want to go from one village to another, I had to have special papers from the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was like 1941, '42?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942, August the 26, I was living at the, at the bakery upstairs in the attic. And I hear some banging. And I thought that my boss wakes me up to come to go to work. So I waited for him, just like usually, he opened the door. All of a sudden, somebody opens the door and puts a light on. There were two gendarme with rifles and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: French?

speaker: interviewee
text: French-- and said, get dressed come with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have much choice. They took me-- it was 3 or 4 o'clock in the morning. They took me to a place. And there were already a whole bunch of my friends there from the children home.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the children's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And also in the village, we had quite a few Jewish refugees. And they were also taken. They took us in a bus and took us to a camp, [INAUDIBLE] [? was in it, ?] not far away from there. There, I met some other people from the other children home. We have four or five children home in the-- in that department there. And I would say we were about 150, 180 men in that, in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men or boys? Mostly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, men and boys. All of a sudden, I found out that anybody who is under 16-year-old will be released. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was one week under my 16th birthday. And I was released. You have to have [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my friends who were over 16 were deported and never came back. So you can imagine how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: How much thanks I had. They were shipped to Drancy at the time. We didn't know what Drancy was at the time. We found out afterward. When this happened, I know that it's time to do something. The Jewish organization took me to a different children home. And the law was that if you are working for agriculture, you still are OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They won't take you. So, they gave me papers say I'm working as agriculture in the farm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you still using your name, Herbert Karliner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at that time-- so I worked there for a while. Then, we know-- we are, we are prepared something might happen in the other children home. And we slept every night all dressed up. Me-- oh, my pillow was a blanket and a coat and something to eat, prepared-- not only myself, everybody else. Also, we had guards every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there adult supervision at these children's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were tremendous, I have to tell you. Those supervision were people, mostly Russian or Poles, who were actually in the Spanish war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russians, Poles, some Germans, who were fighting for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: For Franco?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for Franco-- no, against Franco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Against Franco?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, but in that war, in the-- Franco.

speaker: interviewee
text: That war-- and actually, they lost. And they all came to France. If I can tell you the spirit of those men and women, they raised us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us the basic training for life, camaraderie, and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: They cared.

speaker: interviewee
text: They cared. We lost a lot of them, because they cared. They cared for us more than for their own life. We had, like I said, guards in front of the castle and on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the same castle or a different one?

speaker: interviewee
text: A different one-- but also, not far away from-- the same organization. One day, sure enough, the police came. And the people warned us. And we all run in the woods. They caught a few of us, but not-- very few. The French Underground and the organization got together and gave us false paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your brother during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother was already in a different home. And he got false papers before me. But we always were in touch. And they gave use false paper. And they said, we're going to send you to Spain. And through Spain, you go to North Africa and maybe to Palestine. They gave us some money. And we left for Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: We? Who's we?

speaker: interviewee
text: A whole group of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: A whole group of you, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- we put two boys, two boys in each compartment. We were [? in a ship. ?] I was with a very good friend of mine who is still alive in Paris. We were close friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a train-- and we were inspected by the German [GERMAN SPEECH] army. The [GERMAN SPEECH] army, they had the metal things there. And they were very tough guys. Well, my papers, I had a very strong German accent. And my paper says I came from [INAUDIBLE], which is they speak German and French. So it was OK. I remember I was blonde, blue eyes, didn't look Jewish at all. And they inspect my papers. And I didn't have no problem, my friend either. But the next, next compartment, we had a couple of boys. I don't know if there paper was not so good or what had happened, but I found out that they were not pleased with their papers. And they asked the boys to take their pants off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train-- when he saw that the boys were circumcised, they grabbed them, put--

speaker: interviewer
text: Handcuffs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Handcuffs on them-- and I still saw them. And it was in Montauban, France, where they got him and took him out the train. And I saw him the last time from those boys. Anyway, we came to a place where we supposed to meet some other boys and to get over the Spanish border. When we got there, everybody was gone, because there was a rifle. The German came there already before. But everybody was-- they found out that somebody is-- the Germans going to come. So they all went away. Then I found out that we shouldn't go to Spain, because the Spain, they won't let you go through. You get caught, you-- they sent you right back. So the organization say, try to go to Switzerland. Again, they gave us some money. And we went to the border, Swiss, Swiss border. When we arrived in Leone, the organization told us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we cannot go through to Switzerland. Because Swiss don't-- send everybody back to France over 16-year-old. They have too many refugees there. So we are stuck in Leone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're stuck in Leone. Let's--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a good place to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know Leone.
